coredata.dc:title: Designing advanced 0D/2D heterojunctions of N, S-co-doped carbon dots/Ti3C2Tx MXene nanosheets in electrochemical sensor applications

coredata.dc:description: Ti3C2Tx MXene has demonstrated well-established sensitivity in electrochemical sensing but is susceptible to oxidation. Nitrogen-sulfur doped carbon dots (N, S-CDs) were functionalized on Ti3C2Tx MXene nanosheets via a surface modification strategy, leading to significant enhancement in the performance of Ti3C2Tx MXene. The incorporation of N, S-CDs into Ti3C2Tx MXene nanosheets effectively prevents the aggregation of Ti3C2Tx MXene flakes, resulting in improvement of the interlayer spacing to 1.578 nm and a threefold expansion of the specific surface area. Furthermore, the N, S-CDs contribute to increase the effective active surface area of the Ti3C2Tx MXene electrode, facilitating accelerated the electron transfer rates and providing additional active sites. On this basis, a galvanic sensor for dopamine (DA) detection was attained through loading N, S-CDs/Ti3C2Tx on glassy carbon electrodes. The optimized sensor achieved a linear detection range of 1 μM–1000 μM for DA, with a detection limit of 0.91 μM (S/N = 3). This work presents a promising approach for the utilization of chemically stable Ti3C2Tx MXene and MXene-based sensors, offering a new avenue for quantitative detection of DA that holds potential for application in real sample analysis.

head.ce:title: Designing advanced 0D/2D heterojunctions of N, S-co-doped carbon dots/Ti3C2Tx MXene nanosheets in electrochemical sensor applications

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Ti3C2Tx MXene has demonstrated well-established sensitivity in electrochemical sensing but is susceptible to oxidation. Nitrogen-sulfur doped carbon dots (N, S-CDs) were functionalized on Ti3C2Tx MXene nanosheets via a surface modification strategy, leading to significant enhancement in the performance of Ti3C2Tx MXene. The incorporation of N, S-CDs into Ti3C2Tx MXene nanosheets effectively prevents the aggregation of Ti3C2Tx MXene flakes, resulting in improvement of the interlayer spacing to 1.578 nm and a threefold expansion of the specific surface area. Furthermore, the N, S-CDs contribute to increase the effective active surface area of the Ti3C2Tx MXene electrode, facilitating accelerated the electron transfer rates and providing additional active sites. On this basis, a galvanic sensor for dopamine (DA) detection was attained through loading N, S-CDs/Ti3C2Tx on glassy carbon electrodes. The optimized sensor achieved a linear detection range of 1 μM–1000 μM for DA, with a detection limit of 0.91 μM (S/N = 3). This work presents a promising approach for the utilization of chemically stable Ti3C2Tx MXene and MXene-based sensors, offering a new avenue for quantitative detection of DA that holds potential for application in real sample analysis.

ce:section.ce:para: Ti3C2Tx MXene nanosheets have shown potential for practical applications in electrochemical analysis owing to their exceptional electrical properties, surface area, and hydrophilic functional groups, making them ideal materials for electrochemical sensors [1–5]. They have been successfully utilized for the detection of variable compounds, including inorganic small molecule compounds, organic pesticides, bioactive molecules, and other substances, especially for dopamine (DA) [6–12]. Unfortunately, Ti3C2Tx MXene nanosheets are subject to challenges such as spontaneous oxidation and strong intermolecular forces that lead to self-stacking, making the direct use in electrodes construction less favorable [13]. In recent year, researchers have explored several potential materials (i.e., metal nanoparticles, organic quantum dots, metal-organic backbones, graphene) for combining with Ti3C2Tx MXene to delvelp corresponding electrochemical sensors for DA [14–17]. For example, Zhang et al. combined surfactants with Ti3C2Tx to fabricate three-dimensional porous Ti3C2Tx films, achieving a remarkable detection limit (LOD) of 36.8 nM in the DA assay [18]. Subsequently, another study by Zhang et al. involved the synthesis of Ti3C2Tx-ERHG by incorporating electrochemically reduced holey graphene (ERHG) with Ti3C2Tx and used it for modifying glassy carbon electrodes in DA detection [19]. The Ti3C2Tx-ERHG sensor showed excellent sensitivity for the DA detection in serum containing bovine serum albumin. Saptami Suresh Shetty et al. prepared Fe-SACs/Ti3C2Tx by anchored Ti3C2Tx nanosheets with iron single atom catalysts (Fe-SACs) and used them for the determination of DA [20]. Fe-SACs/Ti3C2Tx has an LOD of 1.0 nM for DA and can track DA production in situ in PC12 cells. It may be a diagnostic platform to assess tumor progression and the quality of cellular DA communication. Nevertheless, these MXene-based sensors present well detection performance, but they have limitations related to toxicity, synthesis route, and cost. To overcome these drawbacks, the integration of carbon dots (CDs) with Ti3C2Tx MXene is considered a potential option to improve the stability of Ti3C2Tx MXene and enhance the response in electrochemical analysis. This approach aims to address the current limitations associated with other materials used in the MXene-based electrochemical sensors.

CDs are nanomaterials with size smaller than 10 nm, known for their excellent electrical conductivity, chemical stability, easy modification, and low toxicity. They have been extensively utilized in a variety of fields, such as electrochemical sensing, biosensing, and electrocatalysis [21–24]. Nitrogen-sulfur doped CDs (N, S-CDs) have been developed to increase the conductivity of CDs [25]. The incorporation of nitrogen can improve the hydrophilicity and electron migration of CDs [26], while sulfur doping increases the distance between carbon layers of CDs, enhancing the electrical conductivity [21,27]. In previous studies, it was observed that CDs prepared with the involvement of cyclodextrins (β-CD) possess abundant active sites [23,28]. β-CD facilitates analytes binding on the CDs surface via host-guest interactions, increasing the likelihood of detecting and catalytically oxidizing analytes. In this study, N, S-CDs were obtained from β-CD and glutathione through a hydrothermal reaction. The quantum size effect of N, S-CDs enables them to serve as effective electrode modifiers, providing Ti3C2Tx with additional active sites and enhancing the reaction rate [29]. Moreover, the functional groups upon the surface of N, S-CDs, like carboxyl and amino groups, allow for covalent functionalization of Ti3C2Tx, further expanding its capabilities.

Dopamine (DA) regulates various physiological processes in the neural system and play a crucial role in kidney and cardiovascular functions in humans [30]. Imbalances of DA levels in biological fluids are associated with diseases, such as schizophrenia and Parkinson's disease [31,32]. Therefore, DA assays hold significant research value for early disease diagnosis. Currently, various methodologies, including fluorescence, colorimetric and photoelectrochemical methods, as well as capillary electrophoresis, are employed for DA detection [33–35]. However, these approaches often require sophisticated equipment, specialized operators, and tedious pretreatment procedures, are not suitable for rapid diagnosis [36]. In contrast, electrochemistry is the preferred method for detecting DA owing to the presence of easily oxidized polyphenol structures [37]. The performance of electrochemical sensors is highly dependent on the modifying electrode materials. Hence, there is a pressing need to design and synthesize novel electrode modification materials that are cost-effective. MXene-based electrochemical sensors offer advantage due to their surface abundance of hydrophilic functional groups, which facilitates rapid binding to DA for efficient detection.

Herein, we employed a chemical functionalization approach to modify the Ti3C2Tx nanosheets through using N, S-CDs. The modification process involved interactions with the hydroxyl groups of Ti3C2Tx nanosheets surface and the carboxyl groups on the N, S-CDs. Afterwards, the obtained N, S-CDs/Ti3C2Tx-modified electrodes were utilized to fabricate novel electrochemical sensors for DA, as illustrated on Scheme 1 . In the N, S-CDs/Ti3C2Tx structure, N, S-CDs were uniformly distributed between the layer spacing of Ti3C2Tx, effectively preventing the aggregation of Ti3C2Tx. Additionally, the β-CD structure on the surface of N, S-CDs/Ti3C2Tx, and its abundant functional groups endow it with supramolecular recognition properties, leading to the creation of numerous active sites for DA loading and improved sensor selectivity. As a result, the developed electrochemical sensor displayed favorable selectivity and sensitivity for DA detection, along with excellent reproducibility and stability. The proposed methodology demonstrated satisfactory performance for detecting DA in real specimens.

Aluminum titanium carbide (Ti3AlC2), β-CD, glutathione were purchased from Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Company. Hydrofluoric acid (HF), 1-(3-Dimethylaminopropyl)-3-ethylcarbodiimide hydrochloride (EDC•HCl), tetramethylammonium hydroxide (TMAOH), N-Hydroxysuccinimide (NHS), threonine (Thr), urea (UA), alanine (Ala), ascorbic acid (AA), glutamic acid (Glu) were obtained from Shanghai Aladdin Biotechnology Co., Ltd. Na2HPO4, K4Fe(CN)6, NaH2PO4, K3Fe(CN)6 were acquired from Tianjin Guangfu Technology Development Co., Ltd. KCl, KBr, Al(NO3)3, Mg(NO3)2, KNO3, CaCl2, MgCl2, NaCl, BaCl2 were bought from Tianjin Fengchuan Chemical Reagents Factory. All reagents were analytically pure grade, and deionized water was used during the process.

The morphology of N, S-CDs/Ti3C2Tx was observed using a thermal field emission scanning electron microscope (Gemini SEM 500) and a transmission electron microscope (TEM, Hitachi H7650). Changes in Fourier Transform Infrared (FT-IR) spectra were documented on a TENSOR37 spectrometer. X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analyses were performed on the D8 Discover X-ray diffractometer, NEXSA spectrometer, respectively, to analyze the crystal structure and elemental composition. All relevant electrochemical experiments were carried out on a CHI660E manufactured by Shanghai Chenhua Instrument Company, China. The counter electrode was a platinum wire electrode, the reference electrode was a saturated calomel electrode (SCE), and the working electrode was a 3 mm glassy carbon electrode (GCE) modified with N, S-CDs/Ti3C2Tx.

Added 1.0 g of Ti3AlC2 powder to 10.0 mL of 40 % HF and stirred for 24 h. The precipitate was washed several times with distilled water to a pH of about 6.0. It was then placed in 10.0 mL TMAOH and reacted for 24 h. Distilled water was washed repeatedly before the pH approached 7.0. The product was then scattered in 20.0 mL of ultrapure water and sonicated over 2 h. The exfoliated Ti3C2Tx nanosheets were collected by centrifugation and freeze-dried.

N, S-CDs were done using 0.5 g of β-CD and 0.5 g of glutathione in 30.0 mL of deionized water at 140 °C for 6 h. Once cooled to room temperature, the mixed solution was centrifuged to eliminate unreacted material. The obtained solution was purified via a dialysis bag (500 Da) over 48 h to separate insoluble impurities. Finally, white solid N, S-CDs were available via rotary evaporator. Meanwhile, 0.5 g of β-CD under the same conditions synthesized CDs.

Hybrid N, S-CDs/Ti3C2Tx composites were obtained via condensation reaction [38]. 45.0 mg EDC•HCl, 30.0 mg NHS, and 20.0 mg N, S-CDs in a solvent of 7.0 mL H2O and 3.0 mL DMSO were stirred for 1 h to activate the carboxyl groups on the surface of N, S-CDs under room temperature. 5 mL of 4 mg/mL in Ti3C2Tx solution was mixed with the above solution and reacted for 24 h. N, S-CDs/Ti3C2Tx can be obtained by centrifugation with several washes and freeze-drying. Also, CDs/Ti3C2Tx were synthesized from CDs and Ti3C2Tx under the same conditions.

The GCE was burnished with aluminum oxide powder until a mirror finish appears. Used ethanol and deionized water to clean the electrode. Dispersed 2.0 mg of N, S-CDs/Ti3C2Tx powder in 1.0 mL deionized water and sonicated to make a uniform suspension. Finally, 6 μL of the solution was put on the polished GCE surface to be dried under an infrared lamp. GCE decorated with Ti3C2Tx, N, S-CDs, and CDs/Ti3C2Tx was fabricated by a similar procedure.

The microstructures of Ti3AlC2, Ti3C2Tx, N, S-CDs and N, S-CDs/Ti3C2Tx were observed via SEM, TEM and HRTEM. Ti3AlC2 is observed as a bulk material (Fig. 1 a). Ti3C2Tx has an accordion-like multilayered structure (Fig. 1b) with very thin lamellar layers, wrinkled surface that provides more active sites (Fig. 1d) [39,40]. The characteristic lattice parameter of Ti3C2Tx is about 0.35 nm, corresponding to the (100) crystal plane of Ti3C2Tx, which is consistent with that previously reported by Li (Fig. 1f) [41]. Fig. 1e shows N, S-CDs with an average diameter of 4.3 ± 0.7 nm (Fig. S1), and a lattice spacing of 0.21 nm is observed in the HRTEM image. Following the introduction of N, S-CDs into Ti3C2Tx nanosheets, the obtained N, S-CDs/Ti3C2Tx exhibit a relatively loose multilayer structure with a surface became rough (Fig. 1c and f). This change is likely due to esterification of the carboxyl group of N, S-CDs with the hydroxyl group of Ti3C2Tx, resulting in the formation of stable multilayers of N, S-CDs/Ti3C2Tx nanosheets [38]. Simultaneously, N, S-CDs serve as an intercalation material to increase the distance between Ti3C2Tx sheets.

The functional groups and interactions between Ti3C2Tx, N, S-CDs, and N, S-CDs/Ti3C2Tx were identified utilizing FT-IR spectroscopy. In the Ti3AlC2 spectrum (Fig. 2 a), the peaks at 3407 cm−1 and 1642 cm−1 are ascribed to O–H stretching vibration and carboxyl group. For Ti3C2Tx nanosheets, the peaks at 1395 cm−1 correspond to C–F stretching vibrations, while the peaks near 3421 cm−1 and 1624 cm−1 indicate asymmetric stretching and bending vibrations of O–H due to water absorption [13]. In the spectrograms of N, S-CDs, the absorption peaks at 3415 cm−1, 1673 cm−1, 1402 cm−1 and 1032 cm−1 are associated to O–H, CO/CC, C–H, and C–O groups, respectively [42]. In addition, weak absorption peaks at 1166 cm−1 and 1070 cm−1 represent stretching vibrations of C–S and C–N bonds [43]. New peaks emerge were found at 840 cm−1 and 1640 cm−1 for N, S-CDs/Ti3C2Tx, indicating the formation of Ti–O–C bonds and N–CO bonds [44]. The stretching vibrational peaks of C–N, C–S appear at 1268 cm−1, 617 cm−1 [45]. These results further verify successful incorporation of N, S-CDs into Ti3C2Tx flakes.

XRD measurements of the crystal structures of Ti3AlC2, Ti3C2Tx, N, S-CDs, and N, S-CDs/Ti3C2Tx were carried out. In comparison to Ti3AlC2 in Fig. 2b, Ti3C2Tx exhibits the disappearance of the characteristic peak at 39° corresponding to (104) and a leftward shift of the (002) peak of 9.5°–6.1°. These changes reveal the successful etching of the Al layer from Ti3AlC2, verifying the transition from bulk Ti3AlC2 to Ti3C2Tx [5]. There is a broad diffraction peak of N, S-CDs at 19.8°, indicating that N, S-CDs have decent crystallinity [42] and in accordance with the TEM results. The characteristic peaks of N, S-CDs are hardly visible in N, S-CDs/Ti3C2Tx, which may be due to the lower intensity of the diffraction peaks of N, S-CDs relative to Ti3C2Tx [46]. The peaks of N, S-CDs/Ti3C2Tx at 2θ of 6.8°, 18.2°, 30.9°, 35.6°, and 61.6° correspond to the (002), (004), (008), (101), and (110) lattice planes of Ti3C2Tx MXene, respectively [41,47–49]. The interlayer spacing (d) of Ti3C2Tx is determined using the Bragg equation:
2 d s i n = n
[44]. The results show that the layer spacings of Ti3AlC2 and Ti3C2Tx are 0.934 nm and 1.301 nm, respectively. Simultaneously, the (002) peak of N, S-CDs/Ti3C2Tx is further shifted to 5.6° and the layer spacing is increased to 1.578 nm. These observations demonstrate that N, S-CDs have been successfully inserted between the layers of Ti3C2Tx nanosheets [50]. The good agreement with the TEM results further suggests that the N, S-CDs should be located between Ti3C2Tx stacks rather than between individual layers [41]. The specific surface area and pore structure of N, S-CDs/Ti3C2Tx composites were analyzed by N2 adsorption/desorption in Fig. 2c and d. The specific surface areas of Ti3C2Tx MXene and N, S-CDs/Ti3C2Tx were 6.194 m2/g, and 18.0546 m2/g, while the average pore diameters were 3.787 nm and 3.885 nm, respectively. As a result, the specific surface area of Ti3C2Tx MXene modified by N, S-CDs was greatly increased, supplying more active sites for the analytes to enhance the detection performance.

To further insight into the componential and structural changes of the samples, XPS analysis was performed. The main peaks at 284.3, 457.2, 533.1, 399.7, and 165.5 eV represent C 1s, Ti 2p, O 1s, N 1s, and S 2p, respectively (Fig. 2e). The presence of N and S elements indicates the successful incorporation of N, S-CDs into Ti3C2Tx, and the corresponding elemental percentages are shown in Table S1. The content of N and S elements intuitively reflects the changes in N, S-CDs functionalized Ti3C2Tx. To better illustrate the successful functionalization of Ti3C2Tx by N, S-CDs, the high-resolution spectra of the constituent elements in Ti3C2Tx and N, S-CDs/Ti3C2Tx were further analyzed. The C 1s of N, S-CDs/Ti3C2Tx were divided into five small peaks at 281.9, 282.5, 285.2, 286.5, and 288.9 eV, which can be attributed to C–Ti, C–Ti–O, C–C/C–H, C–N/C–S, and N–CN/OC–O bonds [51] (Fig. S2a). The O 1s signal at 529.8, 531.5, and 532.9 eV was deconvoluted into five peaks corresponding to O–Ti, OC, and H–O–C, respectively [52] (Fig. S2b). Fig. 2f displays the XPS spectrum of the Ti 2p orbital, where the binding energy peaks of Ti 2p3/2 at 455.1, 455.8, and 456.8 eV correspond to Ti–O, Ti–C, and Ti2+/Ti3+, respectively [53]. The presence of a peak at 459.2 eV indicates the formation of TiO2 species due to surface oxidation during the synthesis process [54]. The peaks observed at 461.6 eV and 464.4 eV in the Ti 2p1/2 region can be assigned to C–Ti–O and Ti–O, respectively [53]. The absence of a Ti–Al signal indicates the successful removal of Al during the etching process [8]. Compared to Ti3C2Tx alone, Ti 2p peaks of N, S-CDs/Ti3C2Tx are slightly shifted, further confirming the reaction of N, S-CDs with Ti3C2Tx. The N 1s can be classified into peaks at 399.7, 400.6, and 402.4 eV representing pyrrolic N, N–C, and N+-C, respectively [25], which is consistent with the IR results (Fig. S2c). Additionally, the S 2p can be decomposed into peaks corresponding to S–Ti, S–C, and SO3 2−(2p1/2) at 159.4, 164.1and 169.4 eV, respectively [52,55] (Fig. S2d). The presence of N 1s and S 2p peaks serves as evidence for the successful incorporation of N, S-CDs on the surface and interlayer of Ti3C2Tx. In light of the above SEM, TEM, XRD and FTIR results, it can be concluded that N, S-CDs were successfully modified on the Ti3C2Tx nanosheets to prepare N, S-CDs/Ti3C2Tx.

Cyclic voltammetric curves (CV) were measured for the variously modified electrodes in 0.1 M KCl and 5 mM [Fe(CN)6]3-/4- solutions. As illustrated in Fig. 3 a, the order of the anodic current peaks was as follows: GCE < N, S-CDs/GCE < Ti3C2Tx/GCE < CDs/Ti3C2Tx/GCE < N, S-CDs/Ti3C2Tx/GCE. The redox peak of N, S-CDs/Ti3C2Tx/GCE exhibited a significant enhancement compared to bare GCE. This enhancement can be mainly ascribed to the high conductivity and large specific surface area of Ti3C2Tx [49]. Moreover, the peak current of N, S-CDs/Ti3C2Tx/GCE was found to be higher than that of Ti3C2Tx/GCE and CDs/Ti3C2Tx/GCE, indicating that the intercalation of N, S-CDs increases the surface area of Ti3C2Tx. The electron transfer ability of the heteroatom-doped CDs is excellent, contributing to the observed enhancement.

The electron transfer behavior of the N, S-CDs/Ti3C2Tx electrode was further evaluated utilizing electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS), and the corresponding equivalent circuit diagrams were fitted with a chi-square of 0.02254. The diameter of the semicircle in the EIS plot represents the electron transfer resistance (Rct), reflecting the kinetic properties of electron transfer at the surface of the N, S-CDs/Ti3C2Tx electrode [47]. The Rct values were obtained from the EIS data fitting. Fig. 3b demonstrates that the Rct of Ti3C2Tx/GCE (289.3 Ω) is significantly smaller than that of GCE (431.5 Ω), implying that Ti3C2Tx accelerates electron transfer on the electrode surface [47]. The Rct of N, S-CDs/Ti3C2Tx/GCE (57.4 Ω) is notably smaller than that of Ti3C2Tx/GCE (289.3 Ω), CDs/Ti3C2Tx/GCE (252.4 Ω). It implies that the N, S-CDs enhances the electronic conductivity of the Ti3C2Tx electrode, which facilitates the detection of the current signal on the electrode during the experiment. These results suggest that N, S-CDs/Ti3C2Tx exhibits excellent electrical conductivity and has potential as a material for the preparation of electrochemical sensors, which aligns with the findings from CV.

To further characterize the electrochemical properties of the N, S-CDs/Ti3C2Tx electrode, the effective surface area of these electrodes was examined using a chrono-coulometric (Q-t) approach. Fig. 3c and d shows the Q-t curves and the linear relationship between Q-t1/2 for GCE, N, S-CDs/GCE, Ti3C2Tx/GCE, CDs/Ti3C2Tx/GCE, and N, S-CDs/Ti3C2Tx/GCE, respectively. The relationship between of Q and t1/2 follows the linear equations: Q = 76.5691t1/2+2.0824; Q = 101.8647t1/2+3.3638; Q = 223.3215t1/2-19.2042; Q = 245.1186t1/2-19.6762; Q = 282.7866t1/2-20.6799, respectively. These calculations are based on Anson's formula [56]:

The effective surface areas of GCE, N, S-CDs/GCE, Ti3C2Tx/GCE, CDs/Ti3C2Tx/GCE, and N, S-CDs/Ti3C2Tx/GCE were 0.2552 cm2, 0.3395 cm2, 0.7445 cm2, 0.8171 cm2, and 0.9436 cm2 using the Anson formula and the slope of Q-t1/2 curve, respectively. In contrast to bare GCE and CDs/Ti3C2Tx/GCE, the incorporation of N, S-CDs/Ti3C2Tx/GCE greatly increased the effective surface area of the modified electrodes. The addition of Ti3C2Tx and N, S-CDs contributed to a 3.69-fold increase in the active area of the electrode. This increase in effective surface area enhanced the electron transfer rate and improved the response of the current signal. This improvement can be attributed to the high conductivity of Ti3C2Tx and the electron transfer ability of N, S-CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of the electrochemical behaviors of DA on the N, S-CDs/Ti3C2Tx
ce:section.ce:para: The electrochemical behaviors of N, S-CDs/Ti3C2Tx as novel sensing materials for DA were investigated through using DPV assays. The bare GCE had the lowest sensitivity to DA and demonstrated poor electrocatalysis in Fig. 4 a. After modifying the electrode with Ti3C2Tx, the peak current for DA detection was significantly increased. This indicates that Ti3C2Tx facilitated the redox process of DA and enhanced the electron transfer rate at the electrode-electrolyte surface [57]. Comparatively, N, S-CDs/Ti3C2Tx/GCE had the highest peak currents for detecting the DA, outperforming CDs/Ti3C2Tx/GCE. This superior performance might be attributed to the molecular recognition and excellent charge transfer and catalytic ability of N, S-CDs [54]. These findings validate the feasibility of utilizing N, S-CDs/Ti3C2Tx as a promising material for developing electrochemical sensors for DA.

The effects of different mass proportions of N, S-CDs and Ti3C2Tx, ranging from 1:4 to 3:2, on the detected DA are illustrated in Fig. S3. As the ratio of N, S-CDs and Ti3C2Tx increases from 1:4 to 1:1, the sensitivity of the detected DA becomes higher and higher, that the peak current gradually increases (Fig. S3a). The corresponding EIS plots showed the minimum impedance values when the proportion of N, S-CDs and Ti3C2Tx is 1:1 (Fig. S3b). However, as the population of N, S-CDs continues to increase, the sensitivity to DA detection decreased gradually, the peak current decreased significantly. Excessive N, S-CDs may accumulate, resulting in transfer blockage and a reduction of active sites. In the meantime, during the range from 1:4 to 1:1, the corresponding effective surface areas of the electrodes are 0.1405 cm2, 0.3618 cm2, 0.7475 cm2, and 0.9436 cm2, respectively, according to the material composite ratio (Figs. S3c and S3d). This result demonstrates that the incorporation of N, S-CDs greatly improves the conductivity of the Ti3C2Tx/GCE, and expands its active surface area to immobilize more DA on its surface [47]. In this regard, a mass ratio of N, S-CDs and Ti3C2Tx = 1:1 was chosen as the electrode material for this experiment.

It was also investigated the impact of the modified volume on the N, S-CDs/Ti3C2Tx sensor. The peak anodic oxidation of the detected DA increased with mounting modifier volume, reaching a maximum at 6 μL (Fig. S4a). Nevertheless, the peak current decreased when the modifier volume exceeded 6 μL. This decrease can potentially be attributed to the thicker N, S-CDs/Ti3C2Tx film layer, which increases the interfacial transport resistance and hindering electron transport [25]. The enrichment process was studied to increase the amount of DA adsorbed on the electrode surface (Fig. S4b). The peak current increases sharply with time, and after 150 s, the quantity of DA adsorbed on the electrode reached saturated, resulting in a relatively constant current change. Consequently, 6 μL was adopted as the optimal volume for the modification of the N, S-CDs/Ti3C2Tx/GCE in this experiment, and 150 s was determined as the optimal accumulation time.

The electrochemical oxidation properties of DA on N, S-CDs/Ti3C2Tx/GCE were studied via DPV methodology within the pH range of 1.5–7.5. The peak current of DA increased with pH from 1.5 to 6.0 (Fig. 4b). However, the peak current gradually decreased as the pH value increased from 6.0 to 7.5. This decrease is likely attributed to the oxidation reaction of DA in alkaline solution, which decreased the amount of active material on N, S-CDs/Ti3C2Tx. Consequently, pH 6.0 was chosen for this experiment. Furthermore, the peak electrooxidation potential showed a linear regression with pH. The regression equation was determined as E
pa = 0.9426–0.04813 pH (R2 = 0.9909) (Fig. 4c). The slope of 0.04813 pH is quite near to the theoretical value of 0.0591 pH, implying that the amount of proton transfer and electron transfer in DA at N, S-CDs/Ti3C2Tx/GCE are consistent [58].

To obtain catalytic mechanism, the influence of scan rate on the electrocatalytic oxidation of DA at N, S-CDs/Ti3C2Tx was investigated using CV. The redox current signal exhibited a gradual increase with increasing scanning rate within 10–90 mV s−1, indicating a better peak current response of the N, S-CDs/Ti3C2Tx/GCE surface (Fig. 4d). There was a linear relationship between the peak redox currents (Ipa and Ipc) of DA and the scan rate in Fig. 4e. The linear relationships were determined as follows: I
pa (μA) = 1.7271v (mV•s −1) + 139.01 (R2 = 0.9908); I
pc (μA) = −1.6753v (mV•s−1) - 74.5107 (R2 = 0.9909) for DA. These suggest that the electrochemical behavior of DA on N, S-CDs/Ti3C2Tx-modified electrodes is primarily an adsorption process rather than a diffusion-controlled electron transfer process. Additionally, as the scanning rate increased, the oxidation peak potential (E
pa ) and reduction peak potential (E
pc ) of DA shifted towards positive and negative directions, respectively. This observation revealed that the N, S-CDs/Ti3C2Tx-modified GCE electrode surface had well-organized electron transfer kinetic behavior. Hence, the possible redox reactions of DA on the N, S-CDs/Ti3C2Tx electrode surface can be concluded (Fig. S5).

The relationship between E
p and the scan rate followed a logarithmic trend (Fig. 4f). The linear relationships for DA were determined following: E
pa (V) = 0.0283 lnv (mV•s−1) + 0.07233 (R2 = 0.9925) and E
pc (V) = −0.02753 lnv (mV•s−1) + 0.1564 (R2 = 0.9901). E
pa and E
pc adhere to Laviron equation [47]:

Further confirmation was obtained that the reaction of DA at the electrode solution interface involves two electrons and protons [59], which is consistent with the inference in Fig. S5. Scheme 1 describes the possible reaction mechanism of DA. Importantly, compared to Ti3C2Tx/GCE, the surface of the N, S-CDs/Ti3C2Tx/GCE offers a higher number of active sites for the DA reaction and acts as a facilitation channel for enhanced ion mobility.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection performance of DA on N, S-CDs/Ti3C2Tx
ce:section.ce:para:
Basing on the above analysis, N, S-CDs/Ti3C2Tx was found to be an electrode material with a high electrochemical response to DA, making it appropriate for the electrochemical quantification of DA. The current response of different concentrations of DA was investigated using DPV (Fig. 5 a). Peaking currents increased with the concentrations of DA, accompanied by a slight potential shift, demonstrating coverage or adsorption of DA on the surface of the N, S-CDs/Ti3C2Tx/GCE [60]. A well-linear relationship between the peak current and the concentration of DA was observed in Fig. 5b: I
pa = 0.4110C + 7.6989 (R2 = 0.9901, 0–100 μM); I
pa = 0.1006C + 43.5288 (R2 = 0.9912, 100–1000 μM). At higher concentrations, the electrode contamination may cause lower sensitivity. As the concentration of DA increases, oxidation products form and adsorb to the N, S-CDs/Ti3C2Tx electrode surface, impeding DA diffusion and oxidation [61]. The LOD of the method were calculated using the equation as follows [62]:
(5)
L O D = 3.3
S D / b
SD is the standard deviation of 0.1131 for the blank solution, and b is slope of the linear equation. The LODs for DA were determined to be 0.91 and 3.71 μM. Table 1 compares the linear ranges and LODs of DA detection in previously studied electrode materials. The N, S-CDs/Ti3C2Tx sensor exhibited a wide linear range of DA detection from 1 to 1000 μM and a relatively low LOD of 0.91 μM, demonstrating improved sensitivity for DA detection compared to existing studies. This may be due to N, S-CDs/Ti3C2Tx serving as electrode modifiers, providing more active sites that readily bind to DA and contribute to higher reaction rates. In comparison, the N, S-CDs/Ti3C2Tx sensors for DA quantification offers a relatively straightforward modification of the electrode and meets the requirements of DA detection. The sensor was confirmed to have outstanding electrochemical behavior and holds promise as a platform for DA detecting in real samples.

The anti-interference ability of N, S-CDs/Ti3C2Tx was analyzed via adding several potentially interfering substances to PBS containing 200 μM DA. Upon addition of 5-fold concentration of interfering substances such as Ba2+, Ca2+, NO3 −, Ala, Glu, Thr, the peak position was shifted, but there was no significant change of current response in the DPV plots (Fig. S6a). The current loss rate was also within 30 % in the presence of multiple metal ions (DMI) mentioned above at the same time. Even, in the case of UA and AA, which are species with similar oxidation potentials to DA, the current loss is within 20 %. The higher selectivity of N, S-CDs/Ti3C2Tx for DA may be related to the π-π interactions from N, S-CDs/Ti3C2Tx to the phenyl groups in DA, facilitating electron transfer during DA oxidation [63]. The weaker interaction of N, S-CDs/Ti3C2Tx with other interferents leads to limited oxidation, explaining the lower current response observed. Thus, N, S-CDs/Ti3C2Tx demonstrates favorable anti-interference properties and holds promising potential for detecting DA in complex samples. Furthermore, the variation in peak current for eight consecutive measurements of 200 μM DA was less than 10.0 % (Fig. 5c), revealing the outstanding repeatability of N, S-CDs/Ti3C2Tx.

The reproducibility of the sensor was assessed by using six independent GCEs modified with N, S-CDs/Ti3C2Tx to detect the same level in DA. The current signals obtained from the six different modified electrodes remained consistently stable with a relative standard deviation (RSD) of 2.03 % in Fig. S5b. This indicates the great reproducibility of the fabricated sensors. It is worth noting that the poor stability of Ti3C2Tx possess a significant challenge to electrochemical sensors development. The storage stability of N, S-CDs/Ti3C2Tx sensor was studied via storing them at room temperature for 14 days and periodically evaluating their performance. It was observed that even after 14 days of storage, 90 % of the original response current value could still be maintained (Fig. 5d), demonstrating the long-term stability of N, S-CDs/Ti3C2Tx sensor.

To illustrate the reliability of N, S-CDs/Ti3C2Tx modified electrodes in practical applications, we assayed UA in human urine samples, and the actual operational images are shown in Fig. S6. The urine was taken from healthy volunteers at Tiangong university. The standard addition methodology was employed to explore the feasibility of N, S-CDs/Ti3C2Tx sensors for practical applications. The results of five replicate determinations were compared with the spiked amounts of each analyte and recoveries were calculated as listed in Table S2. There were recoveries ranging of 96.78 %–104.85 % for DA spiked in human urine samples. It was demonstrated that the constructed sensor has well utility and accuracy, allowing it to be utilized for analyzing DA in real specimens.

This work presents the first successful synthesis of N, S-CDs/Ti3C2Tx hybrid nanosheets by combining N, S-CDs with Ti3C2Tx through chemical modification. By harnessing the advantages of both N, S-CDs and Ti3C2Tx sheets, this work effectively leveraged their properties. Loading N, S-CDs onto Ti3C2Tx nanosheets prevented material agglomeration and increased the layer spacing from 1.301 to 1.578 nm. The current response of N, S-CDs/Ti3C2Tx to DA was twice that of Ti3C2Tx under the same condition, and the electrical impedance value was reduced to 57.4 Ω. Compared to Ti3C2Tx alone, the incorporation of N, S-CDs into Ti3C2Tx significantly enlarged the active surface area of the electrode to 0.9436 cm2, tripling the specific surface area and leading to improved electrocatalytic activity towards DA. The developed sensor based on N, S-CDs/Ti3C2Tx displayed a broad detecting range for DA of 1–1000 μM, with an impressively low LOD of 0.91 μM. Moreover, the recoveries in the range of 96.78–104.85 % obtained for determining DA in human urine through a standard spiking assay, further validated the practicality of the sensor. Therefore, this work presents an effectively protective means of Ti3C2Tx nanosheets and demonstrates the promising possibilities of N, S-CDs/Ti3C2Tx in electrochemical sensing.
